# UC-10.12.31 · POS Transaction Anomaly Detection

## Description

Spikes in voids, returns, or manager overrides may indicate insider theft or terminal compromise.

## Value

Spikes in voids, returns, or manager overrides may indicate insider theft or terminal compromise.

## Implementation

Compare peer stores; video correlate with exception transactions; PCI-safe tokenization at ingest.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: POS gateway, store systems.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="pos:txn"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Compare peer stores; video correlate with exception transactions; PCI-safe tokenization at ingest.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=retail sourcetype="pos:txn"
| eval is_void=if(txn_type IN ("VOID","RETURN"),1,0)
| stats sum(is_void) as voids, count as txns, sum(amount) as sales by store_id, terminal_id
| eval void_rate=voids/txns
| where void_rate>0.15 OR voids>30
| sort -void_rate
```

Understanding this SPL

**POS Transaction Anomaly Detection** — Spikes in voids, returns, or manager overrides may indicate insider theft or terminal compromise.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="pos:txn"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): POS gateway, store systems. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: retail; **sourcetype**: pos:txn. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=retail, sourcetype="pos:txn". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **is_void** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by store_id, terminal_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **void_rate** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where void_rate>0.15 OR voids>30` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (hot terminals), Map (stores), Line chart (void rate trend).

## SPL

```spl
index=retail sourcetype="pos:txn"
| eval is_void=if(txn_type IN ("VOID","RETURN"),1,0)
| stats sum(is_void) as voids, count as txns, sum(amount) as sales by store_id, terminal_id
| eval void_rate=voids/txns
| where void_rate>0.15 OR voids>30
| sort -void_rate
```

## Visualization

Table (hot terminals), Map (stores), Line chart (void rate trend).
